observations.recommendation: The existence, accuracy and completeness of the Property, Plant and Equipment (PPE) accounts amounting to ₱193.536 million could not be ascertained due to non-submission of Report on the Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment (RCPPE) and property cards and other reports were not updated, contrary to contrary to COA Circular No. 2020-006.

The existence, accuracy and reliability of the reported Inventory accounts totaling ₱420,370.00 as of December 31, 2020, could not be ascertained due to: a) non-conduct of physical count of inventories and non-submission of the Report of the Physical Count of Inventories (RPCI); (b) preparation of stock cards and supplies ledger cards were not made; and (c) purchases of various goods totaling ₱13.593 million was not properly taken up as inventory in the books of Municipality upon acquisition, contrary to International Public Sector Accounting Standards (IPSAS) 17, Volume I and Presidential Decree (PD) 1445, thereby the balance of inventory accounts are not properly presented in the financial statements.

Utilization of the 20% Development Fund (DF) with a total appropriation of ₱11.235 million for the year was not optimized contrary to DILG-DBM Joint Memorandum Circular (JMC) No. 2017-1, thus, depriving the constituents of vital services and benefits that could be derived therefrom.
observations.managementAction:
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (LCE) direct the Municipal Development Council (MDC):

a.	To regularly monitor the status of implementation of all infrastructure projects together with the Municipal Engineer;

b.	To maximize the use of savings from completed PPAs and evaluate the necessity and propriety of the unimplemented PPAs so that it can be utilized to fund other identified priority development PPAs in accordance with DILG-DBM JMC No. 2017-1 dated February 22, 2017; and

c.	To ensure that all PPAs included in the budget for 20% DF should be in accordance with Section 3.0 of DILG-DBM JMC No. 2017-1 and shall contribute to the attainment of desirable socio-economic development and environmental management outcomes of the Municipality. The balances of appropriations for projects not included in the priority PPAs should be reprogrammed to development projects in accordance with the aforesaid JMC.

observations.recommendation: The mandatory positions of the Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Office (LDRRMO) were not properly filled pursuant to Section 12 of RA 10121, and Section 5 of NDRRMC, DILG, DBM, and CSC Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2014-1, thus municipal plans and policies for disaster risk reduction management may not be properly developed and actions and measures pertaining to all aspects of DRRM may not be effectively implemented.

The Municipality had yet to close and rehabilitate its solid waste disposal facility and that it continuously operated its controlled dumpsite which is prohibited under Section 37 of RA No. 9003, thus exposing responsible officials to sanctions provided under the law.
observations.managementAction:
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive, the Municipal Solid Waste Management Board, and all other officials concerned to identify and put into effect a permanent and RA No. 9003 compliant alternative to the municipal controlled dumpsite as disposal area for its residual solid wastes.

We further recommended the closure and rehabilitation of the municipal controlled dumpsite in compliance with the provisions of RA No. 9003.

observations.recommendation: Additional income from local taxes, fees, charges and other imposition could have been earned had the Municipality revised its Municipal Revenue Code to reflect updated/realistic rates as provided under Section 191 RA No. 7160 or the Local Government Code of 1991.
observations.managementAction: We recommended that the Local Chief Executive make representation with the local sanggunian to revise the Municipal Revenue Code to reflect realistic rates to enhance generation of locally sourced revenue and lessen the dependence of the Municipality of subsidy (IRA) from the national government.

observations.recommendation: The Municipality’s Annual Procurement Plan (APP) was not prepared in accordance with the prescribed form as provided under GPPB Circular No. 08-2015, hence all the required information were not presented in the plan.
observations.managementAction: We recommended that the Local Chief Executive to direct the BAC Secretariat that in the preparation of the APP, the prescribed format should be followed to ensure that all the required information is included therein.

observations.recommendation: Unspent/ excess amounts after utilization and completion of the purpose of fund transfers to the Municipality from various national government agencies remained inactive for eight (8) years or more in the books of accounts contrary to Section 3.2 of COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012 and thus, resulted to the accumulation of dormant balances in the Due to NGA account as of December 31, 2017.
observations.managementAction: We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct all officials concerned to either refund excess amounts or request the respective source agencies approval to transfer the unexpended balance to the General Fund of the Municipality together with the corresponding supporting documents: (a) Report of Receipt, Disbursement and Fund Balance certified by the OIC- Municipal Accountant and (b) contract or MOA, if any.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended and the Local Chief Executive (LCE) agreed to direct the:
Creation of an Inventory Committee that will be tasked to properly plan on how to conduct and complete the physical inventory within the prescribed period of all PPEs and ensure timely preparation of RPCPPE;
GSO to conduct inventory of all unserviceable properties and prepare the Inventory and Inspection Report of Unserviceable Properties (IIRUP) to facilitate and expedite the disposal of all unserviceable properties. Moreover, for the GSO to maintain and update the PCs to record all procurement and disposals of PPEs so that a reconciliation between the PCs with the physical count can be made; and
Municipal Accountant and the GSO to conduct periodic reconciliation of the accounting and property records with the physical count and make the necessary adjustments on any discrepancies noted, for accurate and fair presentation of PPE account balances in the financial statements.
The aforementioned audit observation was communicated to management thru Audit Observation Memorandum No. 2022-002 dated March 1, 2022.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive (LCE) agreed to direct the Municipal Accountant to prepare the necessary journal entries to take up the depreciation for the current year, update the related Accumulated Depreciation account as well as record adjustments in the Government Equity Account for PY depreciation expenses not previously recorded.
The aforementioned audit observation was communicated to management thru Audit Observation Memorandum No. 2022-007 dated March 31, 2022.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive (LCE) agreed to direct the Municipal Accountant to:
Review prior years’ transactions, exhaust remedies to secure documents supporting the same and immediately record the reconciling items which persisted for a long period;
Enroll the Municipality’s accounts under the LBP weAccess facility inorder to obtain copy/ies of bank statements at the earliest possible time and undertake measures to address the cause/s of delay in the preparation of the monthly BRS for other bank accounts; and
Submit to the Auditor the monthly BRS of the seven Cash in Bank-LCCA SL balances without BRS, and henceforth ensure that monthly BRS of all bank accounts are submitted to the Audit Team within the prescribed period.
The aforementioned audit observation was communicated to management thru Audit Observation Memorandum No. 2022-006 dated March 30, 2022.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive, the Local Development Council, the Municipal Planning and Development Officer and other municipal officials concerned agreed to look into the cause(s) of the inefficient implementation of the Municipality’s development projects and initiate the appropriate corrective measures.
We further recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to instruct the municipal officials concerned to see to it that full and efficient implementation of these projects should be prioritized to ensure that the Municipality’s constituents would not be deprived of the share in the Municipality’s budget and that they could fully avail the benefits to which they are entitled.
The aforementioned audit observation was communicated to management thru Audit Observation Memorandum No. 2022-011 dated April 19, 2022.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the OIC-Municipal Accountant to submit the lacking documents and ensure that payments made are properly supported with complete documentation.
The aforementioned audit observation was communicated to management thru Audit Observation Memorandum No. 2022-013 dated April 20, 2022.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the submission of relevant documents as mentioned above to support the validity and propriety of the payment of COVID-19 Hazard Pay.
The aforementioned audit observation was communicated to management thru Audit Observation Memorandum No. 2022-004 dated March 29, 2022.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the Municipal Accountant:
a.	To conduct periodic verification and validation of the existence of Due to NGAs and Due to LGUs amounting to ₱3.356 million and ₱4.127 million respectively as of December 31, 2021 and determine if there are concerned source agency with valid claims. Return/remit to the Source Agency any unexpended amount of fund transfer after project completion pursuant to COA Circular No. 94-013;
b.	And other concerned officers to strictly comply with the pertinent documentary requirements such as Memorandum of Agreement/Trust Agreements and take necessary action to implement the unutilized fund transfers; and
To verify items included in the Due to LGUs account and prepare necessary adjusting entries to reflect the correct balance of the said account.
The aforementioned audit observation was communicated to management thru Audit Observation Memorandum No. 2022-009 dated April 5, 2022.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the concerned officials to strictly comply with the guidelines provided for in DBM LBC No. 125 in the utilization of the Bayanihan Grant to Cities and Municipalities (BGCM) specifically on its reporting and reporting guidelines so that the status of implementation of programs, projects and activities could be properly monitored.
The aforementioned audit observation was communicated to management thru Audit Observation Memorandum No. 2022-001 dated February 21, 2022.

We recommended and Management agreed that the Municipality formulate and implement DRRM programs, projects and activities that are congruent with the thematic areas and other policies of disaster risk reduction and management to strengthen and enhance the Municipality’s disaster capabilities at all levels.
The aforementioned audit observation was communicated to management thru Audit Observation Memorandum No. 2022-003 dated March 22, 2022.

We recommended and the Management agreed to organize and fill-in the minimum required position classification and compensation scheme in the Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Office as prescribed by NDRRMC, DILG, DBM and CSC Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2014-1, so that programs and projects pertaining to DRRM will be carried out efficiently and effectively.
The aforementioned audit observation was communicated to management thru Audit Observation Memorandum No. 2022-005 dated March 29, 2022.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the concerned officials and employees to refrain from incurring expenditures which are religious in nature and not related to the core functions of the LGU.
The aforementioned audit observation was communicated to management thru Audit Observation Memorandum No. 2022-008 dated April 4, 2022.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the concerned officials to submit justification for the procurement of the above-mentioned electronic the devices which may be considered as unnecessary expenditure and henceforth, ensure compliance to COA Circular No. 2012-003.
The aforementioned audit observation was communicated to management thru Audit Observation Memorandum No. 2022-010 dated April 12, 2022.

The accuracy of Cash in Bank-LCCA as at December 31, 2020 could not be ascertained due to unadjusted reconciling items totaling ₱1.677 million and non-submission of monthly Bank Reconciliation Statements on three accounts, contrary to COA Circular No. 96-011 and Section 74 of Presidential Decree (P.D.) No. 1445, resulting in the overstatement and unreliability of the cash account balance in the financial statements.

Funds received from the Iloilo Provincial Government (IPG) amounting to ₱7.189  million as financial assistance for COVID-19 were not fully utilized in accordance with its proposed expenditures/project proposal and Memorandum of Agreement (MOA), contrary to COA Circular Nos. 94-013 and 2012-001, thereby the primary purpose for which the fund was intended was not achieved.
observations.managementAction:
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:

Utilize fund transfers received in accordance with its proposed expenditures/project proposal and memorandum of agreement pursuant to COA Circular Nos. 94-013 and 2012-001; and

b.  Submit the criteria used in choosing the beneficiaries for Cash Assistance to less fortunate individuals and henceforth, comply with all policy guidelines and regulations in the implementation of programs funded through inter-agency assistance to avoid disqualification on future projects and programs.

observations.recommendation: Disbursements amounting to ₱3.809 million were not among those Programs, Projects and Activities (PPAs) for which the Bayanihan Grant to Cities and Municipalities (BGCM) was to be utilized, contrary to LBC No. 125, thereby defeating the purpose for which the fund was established and deprived the primary beneficiaries of the desired benefits therefrom.
observations.managementAction:
We recommend that the Local Chief Executive direct the:

a. Concerned officials to strictly comply with all policy guidelines and regulations in the implementation of programs, projects and activities, and utilization of funds received for specific purposes to ensure the proper and judicious application of resources and to avoid disqualification on future projects and programs of the National Government; and

observations.recommendation: Lapses in the verification of the accomplished Social Amelioration Card of the ESP beneficiaries resulted in the payment of benefits to 14 ineligible beneficiaries amounting to ₱132,000.00, contrary to the provisions of DSWD Memorandum Circular No. 09.
observations.managementAction:
We recommended to the Local Chief Executive and concerned officials to:

a.  Require the ineligible beneficiaries of the Emergency Subsidy Program/LGU-Guimbal, Iloilo to refund the amount of ₱132,000.00; and

b.  Assign additional personnel to conduct the validation of beneficiaries in cases there will be additional benefits to be granted.

observations.recommendation: Donations in-kind were not supported by appropriate acknowledgement receipts and reports were not prepared, contrary to COA Circular Nos. 2014-002 and 2020-009, and COA Memorandum No. 2014-009, thus rendering the difficulty in ascertaining the completeness and accounting for total donations received.
observations.managementAction:
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the:

Observe the basic controls in the receipt, recording, inventory, storage and distribution of the in-kind donations received; 

Prepare and submit a Summary/List of Donations Received, Distributed and Balances as well as proof of receipt by and distribution to the beneficiaries for donation in-kind received in compliance with COA Circular No. 2020-009; and

Submit the required reports together with the supporting documents to the Office of the Auditor.

observations.recommendation: Deficiencies in the accounting of cash donations amounting to ₱319,900.00 resulted in the difficulty of tracking the completeness and utilization of the fund balance contrary to COA Circular Nos. 2014-002 and 2020-009.

The desired objectives of reducing disaster risk and enhancing disaster preparedness were not optimally achieved due to: (a) non-updating of the Municipality’s Local Disaster Risk Reduction Management Fund Investment Plan (LDRRMFIP) to contain the programs, projects and activities in the approved supplemental budget and cash donations received; and (b) charging of expenditure items from previous years’ unexpended LDRRMF was not in congruence with the programs, projects and activities in the supplemental AIP, contrary to the guidelines set forth by COA Circular No. 2012-002, Republic Act No. 10121 and NDRRMC, DBM and DILG JMC No. 2013-1.
observations.managementAction:
We recommended that Management:

a. The Acting LDRRMO prepare the Municipality’s updated LDRRMFIP to cover all items included in the approved supplemental budgets, and update the programs pertaining to its unexpended prior years LDRRMF in compliance with Item 5.0 COA of Circular No. 2012-002 and Item 6.0 of NDRRMC, DBM and DILG JMC No. 2013-1; and,

b. The Municipality make disbursements which are consistent with the approved programs/ projects/ activities in its supplemental/ augmented AIP and LDRRMFIP to achieve the objectives for which the fund was intended.

observations.recommendation: The Municipality made inappropriate charges against the five percent Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Fund (LDRRMF), contrary to the guidelines embodied in DBM-DILG Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-1, thereby precluding the efficient utilization of fund with the objective of strengthening and enhancing disaster preparedness and response capabilities of LGUs at all levels.
observations.managementAction:
We recommended that Management:

a. That the Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Officer (LDRRMO) and Municipal Budget Officer see to it that disbursements charged from LDRRMF are made in accordance with the Municipality’s approved DRRM programs; and

b. The Municipality formulate and implement DRRM programs, projects and activities that are congruent with the thematic areas of disaster prevention and mitigation, preparedness, response, and disaster rehabilitation and recovery to strengthen and enhance the Municipality’s disaster capabilities at all levels.

observations.recommendation: The unexpended balance of the Local Disaster Risk Reduction Management Fund (LDRRMF) for CY 2015 to 2019 totaling ₱7.178 million were not transferred to the Special Trust Fund contrary to Section 21, paragraph 3 of Republic Act No. 101211 and Item 5.0 of COA Circular No. 2012-002, thereby, precluding the achievement of the purpose for which the fund was intended and deprived the Municipality of additional funds for use in disaster-related activities.
observations.managementAction:
We recommended the following:

a. Balances pertaining to the QRF and DRRMF-MOOE from previous years’ unexpended LDRRMF be transferred to a Special Trust Fund in the Trust Fund books of the Municipality; and

b. Pertinent subsidiary ledgers be maintained for transfers of agency’s unexpended DRRMF to the trust fund books in accordance with the above-mentioned guidelines.

observations.recommendation: Pre & Post Harvest Facilities/Hand Tractor project through Bottom-up Budgeting amounting to ₱650,000.00 remained unimplemented as of December 31, 2020 contrary to DBM-DILG-DSWD-NAPC Joint Memorandum Circular No. 7 and the Memorandum of Agreement entered into by the Municipality with the Department of Agriculture Regional Field Office – VI, thus, the desired objectives and intended benefits that could have been derived from the program were not achieved.
observations.managementAction: We recommended that Management expedite the compliance and implementation of the project to avoid reversion of the fund to the Source Agency.

observations.recommendation: Fuel requisitions of the Municipality were not supported with complete documentary requirements pursuant to Republic Act (RA) No. 9184 and Government Procurement Policy Board Resolution (GPPB) Resolution No.09-2009 thereby, casting doubt as to the validity and propriety of the transactions.
observations.managementAction: We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the BAC to reconsider the method of procurement for fuel; otherwise, ensure that the supporting documents in the procurement of fuel through Small Value Procurement (SVP) should be complete, valid and properly accomplished pursuant to R.A. 9184 and GPPB Resolution No. 09-2009 dated November 23, 2009.

observations.recommendation: The Municipality was slow/delayed in implementing projects funded by the national government and the implementation of the projects were not properly monitored contrary to Section 2 of Presidential Decree No. 1445 hence, there was no assurance that the desired result and benefits of the projects were achieved.
observations.managementAction: We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Agriculturist and other officials concerned to expedite the implementation of the BUB project.

observations.recommendation: The paid payrolls of the Municipality’s job order laborers were not supported by the necessary documents, contrary to Section 4 (6) of Presidential Decree No. 1445 otherwise known as “The Government Auditing Code of the Philippines” hence, the propriety and validity of the payments of public funds could not be ascertained.
observations.managementAction:
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the head of each department concerned to require the job hires assigned to them to properly prepare their necessary daily time records and accomplishment reports duly signed by their respective Department Heads.

We likewise recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Municipal Accountant to require the submission of the necessary supporting documents from each job hire before their monthly wages are processed.

observations.recommendation:
Procurement contracts through negotiated procurement were awarded to suppliers who did not provide the PhilGEPS Registration number as required under Section 54.6 of the Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184, hence the award of the contract was made questionable.

The Municipality made several disbursements without fully adhering to the procedures provided for by Republic Act No. 9184 or the “Government Procurement Reform Act” and its Revised Implementing Rules and Regulations, hence the validity and propriety of the subject transactions were put into question.
observations.managementAction:
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive, require the concerned suppliers to submit their PhilGEPS registration number.



We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Bids and Awards Committee to familiarize themselves with the pertinent provisions prescribed by R.A. No. 9184 and its Implementing Rules and Regulations and ensure that all the procurements made by the Municipality be in accordance with the same.


We also recommended the Local Chief Executive instruct the OIC-Municipal Accountant not to process payments to suppliers/ contractors in the absence of the required supporting documents.

observations.recommendation: Social Pension of ₱500.00 stipend per month were paid to non-eligible beneficiaries, contrary to Department of Social Welfare and Development (DSWD) Administrative Order No: 15 Series of 2010, thus defeating the purpose of the Social Pension Program.
observations.managementAction: We likewise recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Social Welfare Development Officer as well as other officials concerned to facilitate for the refund of the amount paid to the non-eligible beneficiaries.

observations.recommendation: The Municipality was not able to submit to the Audit Team, the a) January to November 2017 Monthly Pre-Closing Trial Balance for all funds, b) January to November 2017 Status of Appropriations, Allotments and Obligations, for both the current and continuing appropriations, c) Q1 2017 to Q2 2017 Pre-Closing Trial Balance and d) Q1 to Q3 2017 Subsidiary Ledgers, contrary to Section 70 of the New Government Accounting System Manual (NGAS) for Local Government Units (LGUs) (Volume 1) thus, precluding the Audit Team to conduct auditorial review on the mathematical equality of debits and credits as well as uncover errors in journalizing and posting of the Agency’s transactions, if any.
observations.managementAction: We recommended that prospectively, the Local Chief Executive work for the immediate compliance with Section 70 of the NGAS for LGUs (Volume 1), requiring the OIC-Municipal Accountant to submit to the Auditor Team the Monthly and Quarterly Trial Balance and supporting schedules to allow the Audit Team to conduct monthly/quarterly review of the Agency’s financial accounts and transactions.

observations.recommendation: Various infrastructure projects in the total amount of ₱3,878,311.29 were implemented by the Municipality by administration without complying with the Guidelines for Implementation of Infrastructure Projects by Administration as prescribed under GPPB Resolution No. 018-2006 dated December 6, 2006.
observations.managementAction: We recommended that the Local Chief Executive request the Commission on Audit Technical Services to conduct technical inspection on the subject infrastructure projects to validate the actual accomplishment.

observations.recommendation: The Municipality was not efficient in carrying out its Solid Waste Management (SWM) functions, through proper segregation, collection, planning and monitoring which may be attributed to non-strict implementation and enforcement of the pertinent provisions of Republic Act (RA) No. 9003 otherwise known as the “Ecological Solid Waste Management Act of 2000”.
observations.managementAction: The Municipality should establish/ reestablish Materials Recovery Facilities (MRF) as provided under RA No. 9003 to reduce the volume of solid waste being handled by the Municipality. We further recommended to expedite the closure of the municipal dumpsite in compliance with RA No. 9003.

observations.recommendation: Productivity Enhancement Incentive (PEI) at ₱15,000.00 or a total of ₱1,440,000.00 was paid to 96 employees due to error in the interpretation of existing rules and regulations, contrary to Section 1.3 of Budget Circular No. 2014-3 dated December 2, 2014 and No. 3.15.1 of Annex “B”, Section 3.3 of COA Circular No. 2012-003 dated October 29, 2012, thereby incurring illegal expenditures amounting to ₱960,000.00 which reduced funds for the delivery of basic services and development projects.
observations.managementAction: We likewise recommended the refund of the Productivity Enhancement Incentive for CY 2014 totaling ₱960,000.00 paid to 96 municipal officials and employees.

observations.recommendation: Disbursements amounting to ₱2,037,020.16 were spent for foreign travel to Guam, USA to showcase the cultural and performing arts of the Municipality, due to lack of appreciation of existing rules and regulations and contrary to Section 4 with sub-sections 1-2, 4 and 6 of P.D. No. 1445 or the State Audit Code of the Philippines, thus, depriving the local government unit of the much-needed funds which could have been used instead to fund priority projects that are beneficial to its constituents.

Recommendations
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (LCE) direct the Municipal Accountant to:
Review the previous years’ transactions, secure the documents supporting the prior years’ reconciling items and cause the immediate recording of the reconciling items particularly those which persisted in the BRS for a long period;
For current year and onwards, analyze and verify discrepancies/errors and other reconciling items immediately after the preparation of the monthly BRS, and prepare JEVs for corrections/adjustments in the GL to present accurate balances of the cash in bank accounts;
Enroll the Municipality’s accounts under the LBP weAccess facility for early viewing of transactions and to obtain copy/ies of bank statements. For other bank accounts, undertake measures to address the cause/s of delay in the preparation of the monthly BRS; and
Submit to the Auditor the monthly BRS of the three Cash in Bank-LCCA SL balances without BRS, and henceforth ensure that monthly BRS of all bank accounts are submitted to the Audit Team within the prescribed period pursuant to COA Circular No. 96-011 and Section 74 of P.D. No. 1445.
The aforementioned audit observation was communicated to management thru Audit Observation Memorandum No. 2021-004 dated March 4, 2021.

Recommendations
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:
Direct the creation of inventory committee, conduct timely physical inventory and prepare the RPCI;
Require the Designated Supply Custodian/General Services Officer and the Municipal Accountant to maintain complete stock cards and supplies ledger cards, respectively and to reconcile the same;
Require the Municipal Accountant to properly record the purchases of inventories in the appropriate inventory accounts and the consumption thereof as expense, in accordance with the PPSAS 12; and
Henceforth, comply with pertinent provisions relative to the receipt, recording, issuance and distribution of inventories held for distribution.
The aforementioned audit observation was communicated to management thru Audit Observation Memorandum No. 2021-012 dated April 7, 2021.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (LCE) direct the Muncipal Development Council (MDC):
To regularly monitor the status of implementation of all infrastructure projects together with the Municipal Engineer;
To maximize the use of savings from completed PPAs and evaluate the necessity and propriety of the unimplemented PPAs so that it can be utilized to fund other identified priority development PPAs in accordance with DILG-DBM JMC No. 2017-1 dated February 22, 2017; and
To ensure that all PPAs included in the budget for 20% DF should be in accordance with Section 3.0 of DILG-DBM JMC No. 2017-1 and shall contribute to the attainment of desirable socio-economic development and environmental management outcomes of the Municipality. The balances of appropriations for projects not included in the priority PPAs should be reprogrammed to development projects in accordance with the aforesaid JMC.
The aforementioned audit observation was communicated to management thru Audit Observation Memorandum No. 2021-015 dated April 12, 2021.

Recommendation
We recommended to the Local Chief Executive and concerned officials to:
Utilize fund transfers received in accordance with its proposed expenditures/project proposal and memorandum of agreement pursuant to COA Circular Nos. 94-013 and 2012-001; and
Submit the criteria used in choosing the beneficiaries for Cash Assistance to less fortunate individuals and henceforth, comply with all policy guidelines and regulations in the implementation of programs funded through inter-agency assistance to avoid disqualification on future projects and programs.
The aforementioned audit observation was communicated to management thru Audit Observation Memorandum No. 2021-010 dated April 7, 2021.

Recommendation
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the concerned officials and employees to strictly comply with all policy guidelines and regulations in the implementation of programs, projects and activities, and utilization of funds received for specific purposes to ensure the proper and judicious application of resources and to avoid disqualification on future projects and programs of the National Government.
The aforementioned audit observation was communicated to management thru Audit Observation Memorandum No. 2021-008 dated March 17, 2021.

Recommendations
We recommended that Management:
a.	Require the ineligible beneficiaries of the Emergency Subsidy Program/LGU-Guimbal, Iloilo to refund the amount of ₱132,000.00; and
Assign additional personnel to conduct the validation of beneficiaries in cases there will be additional benefits to be granted.
The aforementioned audit observation was communicated to management thru Audit Observation Memorandum No. 2021-001 dated February 16, 2021.

Recommendations
We recommended that Management:
Observe the basic controls in the receipt, recording, inventory, storage and distribution of the in-kind donations received;
Prepare and submit a Summary/List of Donations Received, Distributed and Balances as well as proof of receipt by and distribution to the beneficiaries for donation in-kind received in compliance with COA Circular No. 2020-009; and
Submit the required reports together with the supporting documents to the Office of the Auditor.
The aforementioned audit observation was communicated to management thru Audit Observation Memorandum No. 2021-005 dated March 8, 2021.

Recommendations
We recommended that Management:
Take heed of the basic controls in the receipt, recording and utilization of cash donations received;
Prepare and submit a Summary/List of Donations Received, Distributed and Balances as well as proof of receipt by and distribution to the beneficiaries for cash donation received in compliance with COA Circular No. 2020-009;
Maintain a separate bank account with an authorized government depository bank (AGDB) where cash donations are to be deposited; and
Submit the required reports together with the supporting documents.
The aforementioned audit observation was communicated to management thru Audit Observation Memorandum No. 2021-006 dated March 12, 2021.

Recommendations
We recommended the following:
The Acting LDRRMO prepare the Municipality’s updated LDRRMFIP to cover all items included in the approved supplemental budgets, and update the programs pertaining to its unexpended prior years LDRRMF in compliance with Item 5.0 COA of Circular No. 2012-002 and Item 6.0 of NDRRMC, DBM and DILG JMC No. 2013-1; and,
The Municipality make disbursements which are consistent with the approved programs/ projects/ activities in its supplemental/ augmented AIP and LDRRMFIP to achieve the objectives for which the fund was intended.
The aforementioned audit observation was communicated to management thru Audit Observation Memorandum No. 2021-007 dated March 12, 2021.

Recommendations
We recommended the following:
That the Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Officer (LDRRMO) and Municipal Budget Officer see to it that disbursements charged from LDRRMF are made in accordance with the Municipality’s approved DRRM programs; and
The Municipality formulate and implement DRRM programs, projects and activities that are congruent with the thematic areas of disaster prevention and mitigation, preparedness, response, and disaster rehabilitation and recovery to strengthen and enhance the Municipality’s disaster capabilities at all levels.
The aforementioned audit observation was communicated to management thru Audit Observation Memorandum No. 2021-013 dated April 7, 2021.

Recommendations
We recommended and the Management agreed to the following:
Balances pertaining to the QRF and DRRMF-MOOE from previous years’ unexpended LDRRMF be transferred to a Special Trust Fund in the Trust Fund books of the Municipality; and
Pertinent subsidiary ledgers be maintained for transfers of agency’s unexpended DRRMF to the trust fund books in accordance with the above-mentioned guidelines.
The aforementioned audit observation was communicated to management thru Audit Observation Memorandum No. 2021-003 dated February 24, 2021.

Recommendations
We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to:
Organize and fill-in the vacant positions in the Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Office as prescribed by Section 12 of RA 10121, dated May 27, 2010 and pertinent guidelines embodied in NDRRMC, DILG, DBM, and CSC Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2014-1, so that programs and projects pertaining to DRRM will be carried out efficiently and effectively; and
Make appropriately correct the designation and/ or appointment of the LDRRM Officer.
The aforementioned audit observation was communicated to management thru Audit Observation Memorandum No. 2021-11 dated April 7, 2021.

Recommendation
We recommended and Management agreed to expedite the compliance and implementation of the project to avoid reversion of the fund to the Source Agency.
The aforementioned audit observation was communicated to management thru Audit Observation Memorandum No. 2021-014 dated April 7, 2021.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the OIC- GSO to conduct physical count of PPE annually and update the existing PPE ledger cards and prepare and submit the Report of the Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment (RPCPPE) to the audit team not later than January 31 of each year.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive, the Local Development Council, the Municipal Planning and Development Officer and other municipal officials concerned to revisit the projects funded by the 20% DF and ensure that only those that are needed by the municipality’s constituents are included.  The identification of the projects to be funded by the 20% DF, should include the necessary plan on how to effectively and efficiently implement these projects and minimize describing projects in generic terms.
We also recommended that the Local Chief Executive set up an effective monitoring and feedback system in the implementation of projects to ensure that management and officials concerned are updated of the status and that corrective measures could be immediately addressed on problems encountered.
We further recommended that the Local Chief Executive establish an accountability system in the implementation of the projects funded by the 20% DF to clearly determine person/s responsible for the implementation of a particular project and thus, answerable for its delay/non-implementation.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the acting MDRRM Officer that in preparing the LDRRMIP of the municipality, reference should be made to NDRRMC, DBM and DILG JMC No. 2013-1 to ensure that the plan encompass the thematic area of disaster preparedness as well as the other thematic areas of disaster risk reduction and management such as disaster prevention and mitigation, disaster response and disaster rehabilitation and recovery to strengthen its capability in reducing disaster risks.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive make representation with the local sanggunian to revise the Municipal Revenue Code to reflect realistic rates to enhance generation of locally sourced revenue and lessen the dependence of the municipality of subsidy (IRA) from the national government.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Acting Municipal Treasurer, Market Supervisor and other officials concerned to enforce collection of the delinquent rentals including the applicable penalties and interest.  Failure on the part of the market stall lessee to settle their accounts, the municipality should declare their stalls vacant and be made available to qualified bidders.
We also recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Acting Municipal Treasurer, Market Supervisor and other officials concerned to strictly enforce the guidelines set under the Municipal Revenue Code on the leasing of the market stalls of the municipality.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive to direct the BAC Secretariat that in the preparation of the APP, the prescribed format should be followed to ensure that the all the required information is included therein.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct all officials concerned to either refund excess amounts or request the respective source agencies approval to transfer the unexpended balance to the General Fund of the municipality together with the corresponding supporting documents: (a) Report of Receipt, Disbursement and Fund Balance certified by the OIC- Municipal Accountant and (b) contract or MOA, if any.

We recommended that prospectively, the Local Chief Executive work for the immediate compliance with Section 70 of the NGAS for LGUs (Volume 1), requiring the OIC-Municipal Accountant to submit to the Auditor Team the Monthly and Quarterly Trial Balance and supporting schedules to allow the Audit Team to conduct monthly/quarterly review of the Agency’s financial accounts and transactions.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive, through the Local Disaster Risk Reduction Management Council (LDRRMC), ensure that an LDRRMFP/LDRRMFIP, which should include programs, projects and activities funded by the LDRRMF Special Trust Fund, be prepared and incorporated in the municipality’s AIP.

We recommended the following courses of action:
The Local Chief Executive should constitute/convene the LDRRMC.
The LDRRMC with the LDRRMO prepare the municipality’s LDRRMP/ LDRRMIP taking into consideration the compliance with the minimum standards on disaster preparedness.
The LDRRMO should update/validate its data to the current year and continuously update and validate the same.  Necessary measures should also be adopted and implemented to resolve issues noted during the validation/updating of data.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor intruct the OIC-Municipal Accountant to strictly adhere with Section 4 (sub-sections 1-2, 4 and 6) of the Government Auditing Code of the Philippines (P.D. No. 1445). Further, we require the following:
1) SB Resolution approving the foreign travel and its attendant expenses; 2) Authority to travel for government personel per EO 298 prescribing the rules and regulations on local and foreign travels; 3) Approval of the Municipal Mayor of the travel including the list of the delegation; 4) Justification/explanation as to the status of refund of expenses by Iloilo International; 5) Certified complete list of the delegation together with the cultural performers; 6) Justification/explanation that the performers were enrollees of the government school/s; 7) Certificate of Appearance/Participation on of the delegation certified by Iloilo International and/or authorized official of the government of Guam; 8) Liquidation Reports by the Disbursing Officer on cash advances granted relative to the foreign travel, including journal entry vouchers to take up the liquidations; 9) Submission of documents/requirements to the deficiencies noted in Annex 2; and 10) Justification as to the efficiency, effectiveness and economy of the disbursements relative to the travel to Guam, USA.

      compliance with PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2013-1 as amended.

      on the creation of GAD Focal Point System.

      and formalize  the  monitoring  and  evaluation  of  its  GAD  related

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Municipal Accountant and other municipal official concerned to prepare and submit the necessary supporting documents for the fund transfers.
We also recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Municipal Accountant to require the barangay bookkeeper to prepare the correcting entry in recording the fund transfer received by the barangays from the municipality to take up the same as a payable and not as income.
We likewise recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Municipal Accountant to ensure that requirements under COA Circular 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012 is observed before processing disbursement for fund transfer to barangays.
We further recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Municipal Accountant and other concerned officials to follow up the liquidation of the fund transfers and submit the same for post audit and verification.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Agriculturist and other officials concerned to expedite the implementation of the BUB project.
We also recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Engineer, the Municipal Planning and Development Officer and other officials concerned to expedite the implementation of the remaining projects funded by the LGSF-ADM and all other similar projects and to properly monitor its implementation.
We also recommended that the municipality observe the schedules or timelines regarding the implementation of projects stated in the memorandum of agreement to be entered into by and between the municipality and future source agencies.
We further recommended the submission of a copy of Annex A containing the timelines set by Department of Interior and Local Government in the memorandum of agreement with the municipality for the project Construction of One Unit of Hanging Bridge Project in the amount of  ₱1,000,000.00.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the acting MDRRMO to utilize the 70% of the LDRRMF for projects and activities enumerated in NDRRMC, DBM and DILG JMC No. 2013-1.  For other operational needs of the MDRRMO, adequate funds from the general fund should be appropriated for the purpose.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Acting MDRRMO and other municipal officials concerned to ensure the implementation of the programs, projects and activities charged against Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Fund in order to efficiently utilize the available resources for the intended purpose to which the fund was created.
The Local Chief Executive should also look into the process involved in the preparation of the CY 2017 LDRRM programs, projects and activities to determine the cause of the very low implementation rate and implement the necessary measures to prevent its occurrence in the future.
Prospectively, there should be thorough planning in the preparation of the municipality’s LDRRMFP/ LDRRMFIP with due consideration of the necessity and feasibility of the programs, projects and activities, thus ensuring the same would be properly implemented.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive, the Municipal Solid Waste Management Board and all other officials concerned to identify and put into effect a permanent and RA No. 9003 compliant alternative to the municipal controlled dumpsite as disposal area for its residual solid wastes.
We also recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the concerned officials to expedite the acquisition of the dump site property to enable the municipality to develop the facility.
We further recommended the closure and rehabilitation of the municipal controlled dumpsite in compliance with the provisions of RA No. 9003.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the head of each department concerned to require the job hires assigned to them to properly prepare their necessary daily time records and accomplishment reports duly signed by their respective Department Heads.
We likewise recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Municipal Accountant to require the submission of the necessary supporting documents from each job hire before their monthly wages are processed.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the GAD Focal Point System (GFPS) to observe the provisions of PCW DILG DBM NEDA JMC 2016-1 dated January 12, 2016 and COA Circular 2014-001 dated March 18, 2014.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the GAD Focal Point System (GFPS) to ensure that the municipality’s GAD Plan and Budget be submitted to the DILG for review and endorsement.
We also recommended that the GAD Focal Point System and Municipal Budget Officer include in the GAD budget only those expenses that are allowable to be charge against the GAD budget to avoid future disallowance in audit.
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive, require the concerned suppliers to submit their PhilGEPS registration number.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Bids and Awards Committee to familiarize themselves with the pertinent provisions prescribed by R.A. No. 9184 and its Implementing Rules and Regulations and ensure that all the procurements made by the municipality be in accordance with the same.
We also recommended the Local Chief Executive instruct the OIC-Municipal Accountant not to process payments to suppliers/ contractors in the absence of the required supporting documents.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the MSWDO and OSCA to diligently perform their responsibility/function in the implementation of the Social Pension Program.  The MSWDO should ensure that the final list of pensioner should come from the DSWD pursuant to DSWD Administrative Order No. 15 series of 2010.
We further recommended that prospectively, the Local Chief Executive instruct the Municipal Accountant to make sure that the payment of social pension are based on the final list of social pensioners from the DSWD.
We likewise recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Social Welfare Development Officer as well as other officials concerned to facilitate for the refund of the amount paid to the non-eligible beneficiaries.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive convene with the Local School Board and prepare the three year program for the strategic prioritization of policies in the allocation and use of the Special Education Fund.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the OIC- Municipal Accountant to ensure that the SEF Budget is supported by the required LSB resolution before charges are allowed to be made against the SEF.
We also recommended that the Local School Board prepare the Special Education Fund Budget in accordance with the SEF Budget Preparation Form No. 1 as provided by DepEd-DBM-DILG JC No. 1, series of 2017 dated January 19, 2017.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive convene with the Local School Board and direct the officers concerned to ensure the proper implementation of the Special Education Fund in order to efficiently utilize the available resources for priority programs and projects.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the OIC-Municipal Accountant to recognize the above completed infrastructure assets (road networks) amounting to ₱2,739,547.14 as part of Property, Plant and Equipment in the books of account of the municipality.
We also recommended that disbursements related to the implementation of future infrastructure projects by contract shall be taken up first to the specific “Construction in Progress” account and shall be transferred and recognized as part of “Property, Plant and Equipment” account of the municipality once completed from hereon.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the OIC-Municipal Accountant to transfer the unexpended LDRRMF from the General Fund books to the special trust accounts of the Trust Fund books.

We recommended the following courses of action:
The Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Treasurer to look into the cause(s) of the (a) declining trend in the collections from slaughterhouse operation, (b) decline in the CY 2017 collections from market operation, and (c) the increasing balance of the unpaid market stall rentals.
The Local Chief Executive instruct the Municipal Treasurer to implement measures to correct the noted decline/increase.
Lastly, the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Treasurer to develop plans/program to enhance the local revenue generation of the municipality.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive request the Commission on Audit Technical Services to conduct technical inspection on the subject infrastructure projects to validate the actual accomplishment.
We also recommended that the Municipal Engineer see to it that implementation of infrastructure projects by administration comply with the conditions and requirements set forth in the Guidelines for the Implementation of Infrastructure Projects as prescribed under GPPB Resolution No. 018-2006, dated December 6, 2006.
We recommended the Municipal Accountant maintain a special account in the general fund for the 20% Development Fund as required under Section 313 of the Local Government Code of 1991.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive activate/revitalize the Municipal Solid Waste Management Board and require it to perform its duties and responsibilities.
We also recommended the strict implementation of the provisions of Republic Act No. 9003 in particular on the mandatory segregation of solid wastes.
The Municipality should establish/re-establish Materials Recovery Facilities (MRF) as provided under RA No. 9003 to reduce the volume of solid waste being handled by the municipality. We further recommended to expedite the closure of the municipal dumpsite in compliance with RA No. 9003.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor and all concerned municipal officials and employees see to it that payments for allowances and other personnel benefits should be based on laws, rules and regulation authorizing such payment to avoid suspensions/disallowances in audit.
We likewise recommended the refund of the Productivity Enhancement Incentive for CY 2014 totaling P960,000.00 paid to 96 municipal officials and employees.

We recommend that the Municipal Mayor instruct the OIC-Municipal Accountant to strictly adhere with Section 4 (sub-sections 1-2, 4 and 6) of the Government Auditing Code of the Philippines (P.D. No. 1445). Further, we require the following: 1) SB Resolution approving the foreign travel and its attendant expenses; 2) Authority to travel for government personnel per EO 298 prescribing the rules and regulations on local and foreign travels; 3) Approval of the Municipal Mayor of the travel including the list of the delegation; 4) Justification/explanation as to the status of refund of expenses by Iloilo International; 5) Certified complete list of the delegation together with the cultural performers; 6) Certification by school head/principal that the performers were enrollees of the government school/s; 7) Certificate of Appearance/Participation on of the delegation certified by Iloilo International and/or authorized official of the government of Guam; 8) Liquidation Reports by the Disbursing Officer on cash advances granted relative to the foreign travel, including journal entry vouchers to take up the liquidations; 9) Submission of documents/requirements to the deficiencies noted in Annex 2; and 10) Justification as to the efficiency, effectiveness and economy of the disbursements relative to the travel to Guam, USA.

We also recommend that the Municipal Mayor and all concerned municipal officials and employees see to it that government disbursements should be based on laws, rules and regulation authorizing such payment to avoid suspensions/disallowances in audit.

We  recommended  that  the  Municipal  Accountant  prepare  the   proper
   correcting/adjusting entries to  reflect  the  correct  balance  of  the
   account Cash in Bank, Local Currency Current Account.


   We also recommended strict compliance with Section 74 of PD No. 1445 and
      COA Circular No. 96-011, dated October 2, 1996 on the preparation and
   submission of monthly  bank  reconciliation  statements  to  ensure  the
   correctness of the cash in bank balance reflected in the  books  of  the
   agency.

   This audit observation is a reiteration of previous years audit  finding
   and recommendation.

We recommended that the OIC-Municipal Accountant  submit  the  duplicate
   copies of Official Receipts (O.R.) for the period January 1 to June  30,
   2016.


   We further recommended that all Report of Collections and Deposits (RCD)
   should be supported with the duplicate copies of Official Receipts.


   We also recommended that the Municipal Treasurer  supervise  the  proper
   filing of the reports.

We  recommended  that  the   Municipal   Treasurer   remit/deposit   her
   collections intact  within  the  reglementary  period  pursuant  to  the
   provisions of the Section 69 of Presidential Decree No. 1445 and Section
   32 of the New Government Accounting System (NGAS) for LGUs, Volume I, to
   ensure safety and to avoid possible loss or misuse of government funds.

We recommended that  the  Municipal  Accountant  require  the  recipient
   barangays and Guimbal National High School to account for the funds they
   have received from the municipality.


   We  likewise  recommended  that  the  Municipal  Accountant   draw   the
   appropriate journal entry voucher to recognize a  receivable  from  that
   barangay/national high school for the amount transferred.

We recommended that the Municipal  Health  Officer  explain/justify  the
   delayed distribution of the nebulizers.


   The Bids and Awards Committee should also explain the basis for the  ABC
   in the public bidding and award to MACOR in view of the  fact  that  its
   lines of business do not include the retailing/dealing of pharmaceutical
   and medical articles.


   The OIC-Municipal Accountant should also explain why  the  supplier  was
   paid without deducting the 10% retention money for warranty.

We recommend that the Inventory Committee of the Municipality exert effort to submit the Report on the Physical Count of Property, Plant, and Equipment (RPCPPE) as of December 31, 2014 and 2015 on or before February 25, 2016.

We also recommend that the Inventory Committee see to it that an annual physical count of the PPEs be done and for the Accounting Office to reconcile its record with the General Services Office in order to have a correct presentation of the account in the financial statements. Any unrecorded PPE must be recorded in the books while recorded PPE which do not exist should be verified. In case of loss, the Accountable Officer or the person to whom the property was issued should be held liable.
